nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving blackwater rivers, floodplain lakes, and tributaries with submerged wood, leaf litter, and dense root structures along shaded banks.

decorPreferences: robust or plastic plants (true plants often uprooted)

diet.notes: Generally considered omnivorous with a lean toward plant matter and small invertebrates. A varied diet is commonly advised: quality cichlid pellets as a staple, plus frozen or live bloodworms, earthworms, and prawns, supplemented with blanched vegetables (spinach, peas, courgette) and spirulina-based foods. Live plants are frequently grazed or uprooted.

avoidWith: highly aggressive cichlids (Oscars in undersized tanks, Jaguar, Wolf)
long-finned fancy fish (fin-nipping risk during breeding)

imageCandidates.notes: Photographer Rufus46; 5145x3165. Striking Red-Head/Rotkeil form (Heros efasciatus, sold as Severum) showing the vivid red face, dark vertical body bars and full profile in-frame, sharp and vibrant.
Photographer Alexxx1979; 3300x2200. Gold Severum colour form, whole fish in frame, sharp and well-lit, facial vermiculations visible.
Photographer 'moi meme'; 1600x1200. Classic green/wild banded form showing the diagnostic vertical body bars (the 'Banded Cichlid' look) and red eye, sharp full-body side profile.

summary: The Severum is a tall-bodied South American cichlid with a calmer temperament than most large cichlids. Best suited to intermediate keepers with a tank of at least 250 litres and soft, acidic water. Most fish sold under this name are Heros efasciatus rather than the true Heros severus.
careNotes:
Most fish sold in the aquarium hobby as 'Severum' — including the popular Gold, Red Spot, Rotkeil, and Red-Head ornamental forms — are Heros efasciatus rather than the true Heros severus. True H. severus is native to the upper Orinoco and upper Rio Negro basins (Colombia, Venezuela, and northern Brazil) and was effectively rediscovered in the hobby around 2015; it can be identified by a characteristic partial seventh body band. H. efasciatus is more widely distributed across the Solimões-Amazon and Ucayali drainages and is the species most frequently produced in captivity. The genus also includes H. liberifer, a full paternal mouthbrooder that was long misidentified as H. severus, and the closely related H. notatus. Reproductive behaviour differs across the group: H. efasciatus is a substrate spawner with biparental brood care, true H. severus has been reported to deposit eggs on a surface and then mouth-incubate the hatched larvae (a pattern sometimes called larvophilous mouthbrooding), and H. liberifer holds eggs and larvae in the male's mouth throughout. Buyers who cannot confirm the source of a fish are advised to treat it as 'Heros sp.' rather than assuming species identity.

Husbandry requirements are broadly similar across the complex. All forms do well in soft, warm, acidic blackwater with driftwood, leaf litter, and dim overhead lighting — conditions that reflect the shaded, tannin-rich rivers they come from in the wild. Live plants are frequently grazed or uprooted, so robust species such as Anubias or Java Fern anchored to wood or rock tend to fare better than rooted plants in the substrate. Outside of spawning, the Severum is generally considered calmer than most large American cichlids, but breeding pairs tend to become strongly territorial and will defend their eggs and fry actively. In well-maintained aquariums the species has been reported to live 8–12 years.
breedingNotes:
Pairs form naturally when a group of juveniles is raised together, with a bonded couple becoming apparent as they mature. Sexing young fish is unreliable; in adults, males tend to be larger with more pointed and extended dorsal and anal fins, often develop a nuchal hump (a fatty deposit on the forehead), and commonly show stronger facial patterning (called vermiculations). Spawning is generally triggered by soft, warm, acidic water around 27–29 °C and pH 5.5–6.5, along with the presence of a suitable flat surface such as a smooth slate, a broad leaf, or a smooth root.

Breeding behaviour differs between members of the Heros complex. H. efasciatus, the species most commonly available in the trade, is a substrate spawner — eggs are deposited on the chosen surface and guarded by both parents, with clutch sizes reported at up to several hundred to around 1,000 eggs. True H. severus has been documented as a larvophilous mouthbrooder, meaning eggs are laid on a substrate and the hatched larvae are then taken into the parents' mouths for protection. H. liberifer, which was historically confused with H. severus, is a full paternal mouthbrooder in which the male holds eggs and young throughout. Parental care in all forms can extend for several weeks.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish
primary.notes: Primary source swapped from Aquarium Co-Op — the designated primary returned no Severum content (search 0 results, blog/care URL 404). SeriouslyFish only hosts a complete profile for H. efasciatus (the species most often sold as 'Severum'); the H. severus URL returned 404. Profile used for sizing, water parameters, tank dimensions, diet, and breeding mode of the efasciatus member of the complex.

additional.name: FishBase (Heros severus)
additional.notes: Authoritative taxonomy + native range for true H. severus (upper Orinoco / upper Rio Negro). Lists 20 cm SL max, common ~13.5 cm TL, pH 5.0–6.5, 23–29 °C, and notes larvae are taken into the mouth after substrate spawning.

additional.name: FishBase (Heros efasciatus)
additional.notes: Cross-check for the aquarium-trade species. 17 cm SL, 24–32 °C, pH ~7, substrate spawner with up to ~1000 eggs and biparental care.

additional.name: Fishkeeping World
additional.notes: Hobby-care cross-check: 45 US gal (~170 L) minimum, 73–84 °F (23–29 °C), pH 6.0–6.5, ~19 cm adult, 5–10 year lifespan, semi-aggressive. Notes the genus contains multiple species and color morphs.

additional.name: Wikipedia (Heros severus)
additional.notes: Confirms the trade-name confusion: until ~2015 the mouthbrooding hobby 'Severum' was attributed to H. severus, but it is now recognized that aquarium stock is predominantly H. efasciatus, with H. liberifer being the actual mouthbrooder historically misidentified.

additional.name: Wikipedia (Heros efasciatus)
additional.notes: Confirms H. efasciatus is the species commonly traded, often as a captive-bred gold-colored variant rather than the wild olive-green form.

fish.breedingNotes: Breeding is most reliably induced with a bonded pair in soft, warm, acidic water. The species most commonly sold in the trade, H. efasciatus, spawns on a flat surface and guards the eggs and fry with biparental care. True H. severus has been reported to mouth-incubate the hatched larvae rather than guarding them on the surface.
